answer: Design a tenant-aware dynamic backpressure system using Kafka's native quota mechanism combined with a custom metrics pipeline: 1) DATA-PLANE: Configure per-tenant producer/consumer quotas using kafka.quota.producer.default and kafka.quota.consumer.default, implement quota buckets at broker level tracking bytes/sec per tenant via client.id, use partition-level backlog metrics from kafka.log.LogSegment. 2) BUDGET MEASUREMENT: Track per-tenant E2E latency via timestamps in record headers (_ktimestamp), aggregate p50/p95/p99 latency stats using Metrics library, monitor consumer lag per partition (leo - lso), measure backlog in unacked bytes per tenant. 3) PER-TENANT SIGNALING: Implement JMX-based quota exceed callbacks, use TooManyRequestsException (Kafka 2.4+) for pushback, propagate backpressure via FetchResponse throttle time, signal to producers via produce response error codes. 4) FAIRNESS GUARANTEES: Use hierarchical fair sharing with min-bw-max-fol (bandwidth fair queuing), configure hard quotas per tenant with burst allowance (quota.broker.overridden.default.bps), implement priority-based throttling for Tier-1 tenants. 5) TEST PLAN: 100 tenants with varied SLAs (50ms/100ms/200ms), inject 10x normal burst traffic per tenant, simulate 2 broker failures using chaos-mesh, measure: SLA violations, p99 latency spikes, recovery time, cross-tenant interference < 5%.
explanation: This design leverages Kafka's built-in quota system rather than custom credits. Key Kafka-native mechanisms: quotas enforced at brokers viaQuotaManager handle per-client throttling without application-level credits. Latency tracking uses record header timestamps enabling end-to-end measurement. Backpressure signals use FetchResponse throttle-time and TooManyRequestsException to push back. Fairness uses Kafka's bandwidth allocation algorithms, not basic credit-bucket. Test plan includes chaos (node failures) testing fault tolerance under the KCNA emphasis on operational robustness. Kafka broker quotas are the correct data-plane mechanism, not middleware credit systems.
